courses.title: Engineering Mathematics II
courses.summary: Multivariable calculus, linear algebra, and second-order differential equations — the mathematics that vibrations, control systems, and heat transfer will spend the next three years consuming.
taught_from: MIT OCW 18.02 Multivariable Calculus; 18.03 Differential Equations (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Dot and cross products with their physical readings: work and moment. Lines and planes in space. The geometry underneath 3-D statics revisited with better tools.
Functions of several variables and their partial rates. The gradient as steepest ascent. Sensitivity thinking: which input moves the output most.
Multivariable chain rule and the total differential. Error propagation through a formula — the uncertainty calculation every measurement report owes. Linearization around an operating point.
Double and triple integrals over regions. Mass, centroid, and moment of inertia of solid parts computed properly. Order of integration chosen for sanity.
Vector fields as flow pictures. Line integrals and work; surface integrals and flux. The divergence and curl read physically — sources and swirl.
Matrix algebra and Gaussian elimination. Solving real systems: truss equations, circuit equations, mass balances. Singularity as a physical warning, not just an algebraic one.
The eigenproblem stated and solved for small systems. Eigenvalues as natural rates and frequencies. The concept that becomes mode shapes in Vibrations.
Homogeneous solutions: overdamped, critically damped, underdamped. The characteristic equation as the system's fingerprint. Mass-spring-damper solved completely — memorize this solution's shape.
Particular solutions for harmonic forcing. Amplitude and phase versus frequency; resonance and its danger. The mathematical skeleton of every vibration spectrum you will ever read.
Transform pairs and properties; solving ODEs by algebra. Transfer functions previewed. The s-domain as the control engineer's home ground.
Periodic signals decomposed into harmonics. Spectra of square and sawtooth waves computed by hand. Why a vibration analyst's FFT screen shows lines where the machine has defects.

courses.title: Engineering Dynamics
courses.summary: Motion with forces attached: kinematics of particles and rigid bodies, Newton–Euler methods, work–energy and impulse–momentum — aimed squarely at the rotating machinery this pathway lives on.

lessons.scope: Position, velocity, acceleration in rectilinear and curvilinear motion. Normal-tangential and polar descriptions. Reading motion from data rather than formulas.
F = ma applied with disciplined FBDs. Friction, drag, and constraint forces. Curvilinear applications: vehicles on curves, whirling masses.
Work of forces and couples; kinetic and potential energy. The work–energy theorem as an accounting identity. When energy methods beat force methods.
Linear impulse–momentum; conservation in collisions. Coefficient of restitution. Hammer blows and coupling impacts as plant-floor examples.
Center of mass motion and internal versus external forces. Steady mass flow forces — the jet on a plate that becomes pump and turbine thinking. Rocket-style variable-mass reasoning in brief.
Translation, fixed-axis rotation, and general plane motion. Relative velocity and instant centers. Gear and linkage motion analyzed cleanly.
Acceleration analysis of linkages. Rotating reference frames and the Coriolis term. Where slider-in-slot mechanisms get their surprising accelerations.
Mass moment of inertia — measured, tabulated, and composed. ΣM = Iα for fixed-axis rotation and general plane motion. Starting torque of a loaded drum computed honestly.
Kinetic energy of rotation; angular impulse and momentum. Flywheels as energy stores — sizing one for load smoothing. Conservation demonstrations that survive into turbine trip analysis.
Static versus dynamic unbalance defined precisely. Single- and two-plane balancing logic. Why unbalance shows at 1× running speed — the vibration analyst's first spectral line explained by dynamics.
The single degree-of-freedom oscillator from Newton's law. Natural frequency and damping ratio measured from a decay trace. The bridge lesson into the Year 3 Vibrations course.

courses.title: Materials Science II
courses.summary: The metallurgy that runs a steel-centric region: phase diagrams, the iron–carbon system, heat treatment, alloy and stainless steels, and the failure mechanisms — fatigue, creep, embrittlement — that maintenance exists to outrun.
taught_from: Callister & Rethwisch, Materials Science and Engineering: An Introduction (Wiley)

lessons.scope: Phases, components, and equilibrium. Binary isomorphous and eutectic systems; tie lines and the lever rule worked carefully. Reading a phase diagram as a map of what microstructure is possible.

lessons.t: The iron–carbon system and heat treatment
lessons.scope: Ferrite, austenite, cementite, pearlite; the eutectoid reaction. TTT/CCT thinking, quench-and-temper, hardenability, and the microstructures — martensite to tempered martensite — that set steel properties. The single most load-bearing lesson in this course.
Full anneal, normalize, stress relief, and spheroidize — what each buys and costs. Case hardening: carburizing, nitriding, induction. Choosing a treatment from a drawing callout.
Steel designation systems and what the digits encode. Effects of Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni on hardenability and toughness. Selecting among plain-carbon and low-alloy grades for shafts, gears, and structures.

Austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, and duplex stainless families; sensitization as the classic trap. Gray, ductile, and white cast irons and their fracture signatures. Materials the Gulf's process plants order by the ton.
Aluminum and its temper designations; copper alloys — including the conductor grades drawn at Gulf Cable; nickel alloys for heat and corrosion. Where each family earns its cost premium.

S–N behavior and endurance limits; fracture mechanics in its simplest honest form (K, toughness, flaw size); creep regimes and life estimation. The three mechanisms behind most rotating-equipment and high-temperature failures, with fracture-surface reading as the diagnostic skill.
Thermoplastics versus thermosets; glass transition; viscoelasticity. Elastomers for seals — the O-ring as an engineering component. Temperature and chemical limits that kill polymer parts in service.
Ceramic strength statistics and why tension is the enemy. Refractories — the furnace linings of a melt shop. Fiber composites and where they appear in industrial hardware.
Oxidation and aqueous corrosion in outline; galvanic couples and passivity. Hydrogen embrittlement flagged for later. The trailer for Year 3's full corrosion course.

Property indices and screening; cost and availability as first-class constraints. Substitution decisions when the specified grade is unobtainable — a real procurement scenario. Documenting a selection so an auditor can follow it.

courses.title: Manufacturing Processes II — Machining & Joining
courses.summary: Chips and joints: machining theory and its operations, grinding, and the welding processes — the removal and joining half of the process world, with Kirby's fabrication lines as the standing example.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing (Wiley) — machining & joining chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology (Pearson)
Black & Kohser, DeGarmo's Materials and Processes in Manufacturing (Wiley)

lessons.scope: Orthogonal cutting model: shear plane, chip formation, cutting forces, and the Merchant relation. Cutting temperature and where tool wear comes from. Taylor tool-life equation with its exponents read as economics.
Tool materials from HSS to coated carbides and ceramics; wear modes — flank, crater, notch. Insert geometry and grades read from a catalog. Cutting fluids and their delivery, with health notes.
The core operations with their speeds-and-feeds arithmetic: material removal rate, machining time, and power. Workholding logic. One condensed worked example runs a job from print to cycle estimate.
Lathe and machining-center anatomy; axes and coordinate systems. What G-code programs actually command. Accuracy, repeatability, and backlash as machine-health quantities a maintenance engineer owns.
Abrasive mechanics: grain depth of cut, specific energy, and why grinding burns parts when abused. Wheel designation decoded. Surface and cylindrical grinding with dressing and balancing practice.
Ra and Rz defined and measured; finish versus process capability charts. Residual stresses from machining and grinding. Why a ground bearing journal's finish is a reliability parameter.
EDM, wire EDM, ECM, laser and abrasive-waterjet cutting. Where conventional cutting cannot go: hardened dies, delicate profiles. Selection by material, feature, and tolerance.
SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, GTAW, SAW: how each arc works, its consumables, positions, and typical defects. Heat input defined and computed. The process lineup on Kirby's structural fabrication floor.

lessons.t: Welding processes II — resistance, gas, and solid state
lessons.scope: Resistance spot and seam welding; oxyfuel processes; friction and explosion welding in outline. Brazing and soldering distinguished properly from welding. Matching process to joint, material, and volume.
Oxyfuel, plasma, and laser cutting; kerf and heat-affected edges. Joint types and edge preparation geometry. Fit-up as the quiet variable behind weld quality.
Bolted joints: preload, torque–tension, and why gasketed flanges leak when torqued wrong. Riveting and adhesive bonding in outline. The maintenance view: joints are made to be remade.
The AM process families in one pass: PBF, DED, FDM, binder jetting. Where AM already earns keep: jigs, obsolete spares, prototypes. Honest limits — surface, properties, qualification.

videos.title: Manufacturing Processes II — IIT Kharagpur
videos.note: Machining-focused lecture series: cutting mechanics, tools, and grinding — the video companion to this course's core.

courses.title: Thermodynamics I
courses.summary: Energy accounting with consequences: properties, the first law for closed systems and control volumes, the second law, and entropy — taught toward boilers, compressors, and turbines rather than abstract cycles.
taught_from: Çengel (with Boles), Thermodynamics: An Engineering Approach (McGraw-Hill) — via MIT OCW/NPTEL equivalents

lessons.scope: Closed and open systems; intensive and extensive properties; equilibrium and process paths. The state postulate in working form. Units and sign conventions fixed once, forever.

lessons.t: Pure substance properties and steam tables
lessons.scope: Phase regions, quality, and the P–v and T–s diagrams. Steam-table fluency drills — the skill utilities engineering assumes. Superheat and subcooling located physically.
Boundary work computed for standard processes; heat as energy in transit, not a substance. Path dependence demonstrated. Reading work from a P–v diagram's area.
Energy balance for closed systems with internal energy and enthalpy distinguished. Specific heats and ideal-gas relations. Piston-cylinder and rigid-tank problems solved in a fixed format.
Steady-flow energy equation term by term. Nozzles, turbines, compressors, pumps, throttles, and heat exchangers as one-line devices. The equation this pathway will use weekly for four years.
Charging and discharging tanks; the uniform-state, uniform-flow model. Compressed-air receiver behavior as the working example. Where steady-flow assumptions break.
Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements; thermal efficiency and COP defined honestly. Carnot limits as ceilings no vendor brochure escapes. Refrigerators and heat pumps as reversed engines.
Entropy as a property; the increase principle for isolated systems. T–s diagrams and isentropic reference processes. Entropy generation as the bookkeeping of irreversibility.
Turbine, compressor, and pump efficiencies defined against the isentropic ideal. Real machine performance located on the h–s diagram. The number that separates a healthy compressor from a worn one.
Available work and dead states in outline. Exergy destruction as the cost of irreversibility. Why utilities audits speak this language — a preview of Thermo II.
Otto, Diesel, and Brayton cycles in ideal form. Mean effective pressure and back-work ratio. Enough cycle literacy to hold a conversation about gas turbines — depth deferred to Thermo II.

courses.title: Electrical Fundamentals for Mechanical Engineers
courses.summary: The electrical literacy a plant demands of its mechanical engineers: DC and AC circuits, three-phase power, transformers, and above all the induction motor — starter to nameplate to failure modes.

lessons.scope: The electrical quantities with water analogies used and then retired. Ohm's law and power dissipation. Reading a multimeter without folklore.
Series-parallel reduction, Kirchhoff's laws, and voltage/current dividers. Practical sources and internal resistance. Battery-fed control circuits as examples.
Energy storage in fields; V–I relations for C and L. First-order transients and time constants. Why inductive loads arc switches and need suppression.
Sinusoids, RMS values, and phasor representation. Impedance of R, L, C and series combinations. The 50 Hz world quantified.
Real, reactive, and apparent power; the power triangle. Power factor and why utilities bill for it. Correction capacitors sized in a worked example.
Wye and delta connections; line versus phase quantities. Three-phase power calculation. Why industrial plants are three-phase places.
Ideal transformer relations and real losses. Nameplate data decoded. Distribution transformers in a plant one-line diagram.
Rotating field, slip, and the torque–speed curve. Nameplate literacy: power, current, service factor, insulation class. The machine that drives nearly every pump and fan you will ever maintain.
DOL and star-delta starting; overloads and protection classes. Variable-frequency drives in outline: what they do to the torque curve and to bearings. Motor circuit anatomy from MCC to terminal box.

Shock and arc-flash hazards respected properly; lockout-tagout as a mechanical engineer practices it. Insulation resistance and clamp-meter measurements. The boundary line between mechanical and electrical work scopes.
